quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Equivalence point"?

options.text: The equilibrium distance between two bonded nuclei; decreases as bond order increases: triple < double < single
The point where moles of titrant exactly equal moles of analyte; stoichiometrically complete reaction
The exponent of a concentration term in the rate law; indicates how rate responds to concentration changes (0th, 1st, 2nd)
The rate at a specific moment, determined by the slope of the tangent to the concentration-vs.-time curve

quiz_questions.explanation: "Equivalence point" is defined as: The point where moles of titrant exactly equal moles of analyte; stoichiometrically complete reaction. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "End point"?

options.text: ΔTf = iKfm; solute particles lower the freezing point by disrupting crystal formation
Calculated the same way as K but with current concentrations; Q  K: reverse favored; Q = K: at equilibrium
The point where the indicator changes color; ideally matches the equivalence point; slightly different in practice
The minimum energy required for reactants to reach the transition state and form products; determines the rate's temperature sensitivity

quiz_questions.explanation: "End point" is defined as: The point where the indicator changes color; ideally matches the equivalence point; slightly different in practice. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Titration curve"?

options.text: A plot of pH vs. volume of titrant added; shape reveals acid/base strength and equivalence point pH
The slowest elementary step in a mechanism; acts as a 'bottleneck' that controls the overall reaction rate
One compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances: AB → A + B (e.g., 2H₂O₂ → 2H₂O + O₂)
Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 1.0 × 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C; derived from the autoionization of water: 2H₂O ⇌ H₃O⁺ + OH⁻

quiz_questions.explanation: "Titration curve" is defined as: A plot of pH vs. volume of titrant added; shape reveals acid/base strength and equivalence point pH. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Half-equivalence point"?

options.text: A chemical equation with equal numbers of each atom on both sides, satisfying conservation of mass
The slowest elementary step in a mechanism; acts as a 'bottleneck' that controls the overall reaction rate
The point where half the analyte has reacted: [HA] = [A⁻], so pH = pKa; useful for determining Ka
The number of moles of compound that dissolve per liter of saturated solution; calculated from Ksp and stoichiometry

quiz_questions.explanation: "Half-equivalence point" is defined as: The point where half the analyte has reacted: [HA] = [A⁻], so pH = pKa; useful for determining Ka. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Indicator"?

options.text: The attraction between an ion and a polar molecule; the dominant force when ionic compounds dissolve in water

A weak acid or base that changes color at a specific pH range; chosen so its color change range includes the equivalence point pH (e.g., phenolphthalein, pH 8.2–10, ideal for weak acid–strong base titrations; bromothymol blue, pH 6.0–7.6, for...
A proton (H⁺) acceptor
π = iMRT; the pressure needed to prevent osmosis across a semipermeable membrane; used to determine molar mass of large molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: "Indicator" is defined as: A weak acid or base that changes color at a specific pH range; chosen so its color change range includes the equivalence point pH (e.g., phenolphthalein, pH 8.2–10, ideal for weak acid–strong base.... The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The point where moles of titrant exactly equal moles of analyte; stoichiometrically complete reaction"?

options.text: Crystalline solid
Hess's Law
Ion-dipole force
Equivalence point

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Equivalence point".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The point where the indicator changes color; ideally matches the equivalence point; slightly different in practice"?

options.text: Metallic bond
Activation energy (Ea)
End point
Standard reduction potential (E°)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "End point".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A plot of pH vs. volume of titrant added; shape reveals acid/base strength and equivalence point pH"?

options.text: Triple point
Titration curve
Heterogeneous catalyst
Lewis acid/base

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Titration curve".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The point where half the analyte has reacted: [HA] = [A⁻], so pH = pKa; useful for determining Ka"?

options.text: Stoichiometric conversion
Integrated rate law
Collision theory
Half-equivalence point

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Half-equivalence point".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A weak acid or base that changes color at a specific pH range; chosen so its color change range includes the equivalence point pH (e.g., phenolphthalein, pH 8.2–10, ideal for..."?

options.text: Zwitterion
pOH
Selective precipitation
Indicator

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Indicator".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Titration is accurate?

options.text: Balancing Chemical Equations ensures the law of conservation of mass is satisfied: the same number of each type of atom appears on both sides.
Increasing pressure shifts toward the side with fewer moles of gas.
Zero-order: rate is independent of concentration; first-order: rate doubles when concentration doubles; second-order: rate quadruples when concentration doubles.
Titration is a quantitative analytical technique where a solution of known concentration (titrant) is added to a solution of unknown concentration (analyte) until the equivalence point is reached.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Titration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Titration?

options.text: Strong acid–strong base titrations have equivalence point at pH 7.
Chemical Equilibrium occurs when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates are equal, resulting in constant concentrations of reactants and products.
Balancing Chemical Equations ensures the law of conservation of mass is satisfied: the same number of each type of atom appears on both sides.
Batteries and Corrosion applies electrochemistry to practical applications.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Titration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Titration?

options.text: K > 1 favors products; K < 1 favors reactants.
Weak acid–strong base titrations have equivalence points at pH > 7 (due to conjugate base hydrolysis).
Entropy (S) measures the number of possible microstates (disorder).
Chemical Bonding connects atoms through ionic, covalent, and metallic bonds.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Titration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Titration?

options.text: Electrochemistry and Redox connects chemical reactions to electrical energy.
Atoms consist of protons (p⁺, +1 charge, ~1 amu) and neutrons (n⁰, neutral, ~1 amu) in the nucleus, surrounded by electrons (e⁻, -1 charge, ~1/1836 amu) in orbitals.
The half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration is where pH = pKa, useful for buffer preparation.
Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of reactants and products in chemical reactions based on the mole concept.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Titration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Titration?

options.text: Batteries and Corrosion applies electrochemistry to practical applications.
Titration is a quantitative analytical technique where a solution of known concentration (titrant) is added to a solution of unknown concentration (analyte) until the equivalence point is reached.
K is dimensionless and temperature-dependent.
The relative strength of an acid is inversely related to the strength of its conjugate base.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Titration as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Titration?

options.text: Electroplating using electrolysis: depositing a thin layer of metal (chrome, gold, nickel) onto objects for corrosion protection or aesthetics
Quality control in pharmaceutical manufacturing: acid-base titrations determining the exact concentration of active ingredients in drug formulations
Hard water treatment: Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions forming insoluble CaCO₃ scale in pipes and water heaters when Qsp exceeds Ksp upon heating
Ammonia's pyramidal geometry (sp³, one lone pair) making it a strong Lewis base and critical industrial chemical in fertilizer production (Haber process)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Titration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Titration?

options.text: Aluminum production by Hall-Héroult process: electrolysis of molten Al₂O₃ in cryolite at ~960°C; consumes ~5% of US electricity production
Carbonated beverages: dissolved CO₂ forms carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), a diprotic acid giving soft drinks their tangy taste (pH ~3-4)
Environmental monitoring: titrating water samples to determine alkalinity (buffering capacity against acid rain) and acidity of industrial effluent
The spontaneous rusting of iron: despite being exothermic (ΔH < 0), rust formation is also driven by the entropy increase from solid Fe converting to flaky Fe₂O₃

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Titration.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Titration applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Drug design depending on precise molecular geometry: the 3D shape of a drug molecule determines how it fits into an enzyme active site (lock-and-key model)
Drug elimination from the body often following first-order kinetics: dosing intervals based on half-life to maintain therapeutic blood levels
Vinegar concentration determination: titrating with standardized NaOH; the acetic acid content must be 4-8% by mass for commercial vinegar
Radiocarbon dating using the ¹⁴C isotope to determine the age of organic materials up to ~50,000 years old

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Titration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Titration?

options.text: Quality control in pharmaceutical manufacturing: acid-base titrations determining the exact concentration of active ingredients in drug formulations
Aluminum's amphoteric nature: Al(OH)₃ dissolves in both acids and bases, exploited in the Bayer process to purify alumina from bauxite ore
The spontaneous rusting of iron: despite being exothermic (ΔH < 0), rust formation is also driven by the entropy increase from solid Fe converting to flaky Fe₂O₃
Nitrogen dioxide equilibrium (2NO₂ ⇌ N₂O₄): the brown-to-colorless color change visually demonstrates equilibrium shifting with temperature changes

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Titration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Titration in practice?

options.text: Aluminum's amphoteric nature: Al(OH)₃ dissolves in both acids and bases, exploited in the Bayer process to purify alumina from bauxite ore
Antacid tablet surface area experiments: crushed tablets dissolve faster than whole tablets because increased surface area increases reaction rate
Haber process optimization: high pressure (fewer gas moles on product side, Δn = -2) and low temperature (exothermic) favor NH₃, but moderate T used for acceptable rate
Environmental monitoring: titrating water samples to determine alkalinity (buffering capacity against acid rain) and acidity of industrial effluent

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Titration.
